title: Psalms
author: Multiple authors: David (~73 psalms), Asaph (12), Sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Heman, Ethan, and Moses (1 each); 50 anonymous
date: ~1000–400 BC (spanning the monarchy through the post-exile period)
setting: Compiled over several centuries as the worship hymnbook of Israel, used in temple liturgy and private devotion. Final compilation likely completed in the post-exilic period (~400 BC).
purpose: To give God's people a vocabulary for worship, lament, praise, confession, and trust — covering the full range of human experience before God. The Psalms teach Israel (and the church) how to speak to God honestly and how to wait for the messianic King.

outline.label: Book I: Psalms of David — Individual and Covenant
Book II: National Life and Worship
Book III: National Crisis and Lament
Book V: Praise and the Pilgrim Way

after.label: NT: Jesus quotes Psalms more than any other book

key_people.name: David
key_people.role: Author of 73 psalms across every register — praise, lament, confession, thanksgiving, royal proclamation — and the primary human voice of the psalter

key_people.name: Asaph
key_people.role: Temple musician and author of Psalms 50 and 73–83; his psalms often wrestle with the prosperity of the wicked and God's apparent silence

key_people.name: The Sons of Korah
key_people.role: Levitical singers who contribute Psalms 42–49, 84–85, 87–88; their psalms are among the most lyrical expressions of longing for God's presence

key_people.name: Moses
key_people.role: Author of Psalm 90 — the oldest psalm in the psalter, reflecting on human frailty and God's eternal nature in the wilderness

key_people.name: Solomon
key_people.role: Author of Psalms 72 and 127; Psalm 72 is a royal psalm whose vision of the ideal king exceeds any human monarch and points to the messianic King

context: The Psalms were composed over nearly a millennium, from the time of Moses (Ps 90) through the post-exilic period (Ps 126, 137), and were collected and arranged in their final five-book form (1–41, 42–72, 73–89, 90–106, 107–150) probably in the post-exilic period. They functioned as the hymnbook of the second temple and as the primary vehicle for Israel's corporate and individual prayer. Jesus and his disciples sang psalms at the Last Supper (the Hallel, Pss 113–118), and the early church continued to use them extensively in worship.
christ_connection: The Psalms are the most quoted OT book in the NT, and the majority of those quotations are applied directly to Jesus. Psalm 2 ('You are my Son') is cited at the baptism, transfiguration, and resurrection of Jesus. Psalm 22 ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?') narrates the crucifixion with remarkable precision centuries before it occurs. Psalm 16 ('you will not abandon my soul to Sheol') is interpreted by Peter as a prophecy of the resurrection (Acts 2:27–28). Psalm 110 ('The LORD said to my Lord, sit at my right hand') is Jesus' own primary self-designation and the most quoted OT verse in the NT.

key_verses.note: The gateway psalm: blessed is the one who meditates on God's law day and night — the portrait of the righteous life that the whole psalter develops
'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — the opening of the great passion psalm, which Jesus quotes from the cross and which narrates the crucifixion in detail
'The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want' — the most beloved summary of covenant care, recalled by Jesus in John 10
'The LORD said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand' — the most quoted OT verse in the NT; Jesus uses it to expose the inadequacy of calling the Messiah merely 'son of David'

themes_detail.title: The Full Range of Prayer: Praise and Lament
themes_detail.text: The Psalms give voice to the complete register of human experience before God: exuberant praise (Ps 150), desperate lament (Ps 88), bitter complaint (Ps 44), confident trust (Ps 46), grateful thanksgiving (Ps 116), and penitential confession (Ps 51). What is remarkable is that all of these — including the darkest complaints — are presented as legitimate speech directed to God. The psalter teaches that faith does not suppress negative emotion but transforms it by bringing it honestly into God's presence.

themes_detail.title: The Messiah-King
themes_detail.text: The royal psalms (2, 45, 72, 89, 110, 132) describe an ideal king whose reign exceeds anything any human monarch ever achieved. Psalm 2 describes the king as God's Son ruling over the nations; Psalm 45 addresses him with the divine title 'O God' (v.6, quoted in Heb 1:8); Psalm 72 envisions a king before whom all kings bow and through whom all nations are blessed; Psalm 110 presents him as simultaneously king and priest after the order of Melchizedek. The NT reads these psalms not as hyperbole but as prophecy fulfilled precisely in Jesus.

themes_detail.title: The Word of God as Delight
themes_detail.text: Psalm 119 — the longest chapter in the Bible — is a sustained celebration of God's law as the source of wisdom, comfort, direction, and joy. The psalmist does not experience the law as burdensome obligation but as the faithful friend of the soul: 'Oh how I love your law! It is my meditation all the day' (v. 97). This posture — love of God's word for God's own sake — is the goal of the Spirit's work in every believer and anticipates Christ's delight in the Father's will (Ps 40:8; John 4:34).

themes_detail.title: Suffering, Trust, and the Certainty of Vindication
themes_detail.text: The lament psalms (approximately a third of the psalter) follow a characteristic movement: honest description of suffering, appeal to God's character and past faithfulness, and resolution in renewed trust — even before the circumstances have changed. This pattern is not cheap optimism but hard-won faith: the psalmist chooses to trust God's character in the absence of felt evidence. Psalm 22 exemplifies this: it begins in dereliction and ends in worldwide praise — the cross and resurrection pattern inscribed in Israel's hymnbook centuries before they occurred.
